ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for murtabak. 'Flatbread food product' (FOODON_03315595) is used as the closest parent category. A sardine-filled variant is not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pancakes and similar products) is used as the closest parent. Murtabak is a pan-fried stuffed dough product analogous to a filled pancake; no dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for murtabak.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for murtabak or sardine murtabak. SNOMED CT does not index this regional dish.

serving.description: 1 piece (approximately 1 quarter of a whole murtabak)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sardine murtabak is one piece weighing approximately 200 g, representing roughly one quarter of a whole murtabak as commonly sold at hawker stalls and restaurants in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Murtabak (sardine), cooked — adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central component data for sardine-filled flatbread
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for sardine and dough components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: IMR MyFCD lists murtabak (mutton and chicken variants) with per-100g values. Sardine murtabak is not individually listed. Values are adapted from the MyFCD murtabak entry (closest match: chicken murtabak, ~220 kcal/100g) and cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy sardine (canned in tomato sauce) and plain dough component data. The sardine filling increases protein and omega-3 content relative to the chicken variant. Final values represent a defensible estimate for a sardine-filled murtabak at approximately 60% dough and 40% sardine filling by weight.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of flatbreads and stuffed dough products — analogous estimate based on roti canai / paratha GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sardine murtabak in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. The GI of 57 is estimated by analogy with roti canai / paratha (GI ~57–62, medium category) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the closest structurally similar pan-fried wheat-dough product. The sardine filling does not substantially alter the glycemic response of the dough matrix. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (57 × 49.0) / 100 ≈ 27.0, based on a 200 g serving containing approximately 49 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100g; ~840 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sardine murtabak to occasional consumption, as a single 200 g serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium — about 37% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from canned sardines, added salt in the dough, and condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~57) and high glycemic load per serving (~27)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume sardine murtabak in small portions, as the medium GI of approximately 57 combined with a high glycemic load of ~27 per standard serving can cause a meaningful postprandial blood glucose rise; pairing with a non-starchy vegetable side and limiting portion to half a piece may help moderate glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat, high sodium, but beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from sardines
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sardine murtabak presents a mixed cardiovascular profile: the sardine filling contributes beneficial long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that support cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content (~840 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~6.2 g per serving) from ghee and egg warrant portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sardine murtabak is a pan-fried stuffed flatbread popular in Singapore and Malaysia, particularly at Malay and Indian-Muslim hawker stalls and restaurants. It is made by stretching or rolling a thin wheat-flour dough (similar to roti canai or paratha dough, enriched with egg and ghee) and filling it with a spiced mixture of canned sardines, sliced onions, egg, and chilli before folding and cooking on a well-oiled griddle until golden and crisp on the outside. The result is a layered, slightly flaky exterior encasing a savoury, moist sardine filling with a mildly spicy and umami-rich flavour. Murtabak is traditionally served with a side of curry sauce or pickled onions in vinegar. Nutritionally, sardine murtabak is a moderate-energy food (~218 kcal/100g) providing meaningful protein (~9.8 g/100g) from both the egg-enriched dough and the sardine filling, which also contributes beneficial omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin B12, vitamin D, calcium, and selenium. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~57) and a high glycemic load per standard serving, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated due to canned sardines and cooking salt, which is a concern for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Sardine murtabak is halal-certified at most establishments and is a common lunch or dinner item. It is distinct from mutton murtabak and chicken murtabak, which use different fillings, and from the Indonesian martabak manis (sweet pancake variant).
common_user_queries: How many calories in sardine murtabak?
What is the GI of murtabak?
What is the difference between murtabak and roti canai?
What are the ingredients in sardine murtabak?

confidence_reason: IMR Malaysia MyFCD lists murtabak variants (chicken, mutton) but not sardine murtabak specifically. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest MyFCD murtabak entry and cross-checked with USDA SR Legacy sardine component data. The adaptation is defensible but introduces estimation uncertainty, particularly for micronutrients. GI is an analogous estimate from roti canai / paratha data in the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement for sardine murtabak exists in approved sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD murtabak (chicken variant) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy sardine (canned in tomato sauce) component data. No exact sardine murtabak entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component-level data (sardine + dough + egg + onion) using USDA SR Legacy; these values carry medium uncertainty. 3. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on roti canai / paratha GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for sardine murtabak exists in approved sources. 4. Sodium content is elevated due to canned sardines and is a primary clinical concern. 5. The food_group assignment of 'Grains and staples' is retained from the input record and is defensible given the wheat-dough base constitutes the majority of the dish by weight. 6. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient fields are populated with estimated values from approved component sources.
